content.text: What is Multicloud?

Multicloud is the deliberate use of cloud services from two or more cloud providers — such as Azure, AWS, and Google Cloud — within the same organization. Unlike hybrid cloud (which spans on-premises and public cloud), multicloud involves multiple public cloud vendors.

Most multicloud situations arise from one of two paths: strategic adoption (different teams chose the best provider for a workload) or acquisition (the acquired company already ran on a different cloud). Managing this reality — rather than forcing everything into a single provider — is what multicloud strategy addresses.
Distinction:

Hybrid cloud = on-premises + public cloud. Multicloud = multiple public clouds. Many enterprises run both simultaneously — hybrid-multicloud — with some on-premises infrastructure plus workloads split across Azure, AWS, and GCP.

attrs.alt: Multicloud architecture showing Azure as primary cloud alongside AWS and GCP workloads, unified by Azure Arc management plane
attrs.title: Multicloud Architecture with Azure Arc

content.text: Why Organizations Run Multicloud
The motivations vary widely. Understanding which one applies to your organization determines the right multicloud strategy.
Motivation
Example
Implication
Best-of-breed services
BigQuery for analytics, Azure OpenAI for LLMs, AWS S3 for object storage
Intentional — manage integration complexity for service quality
Vendor risk / lock-in avoidance
SLA breach or price increase by one provider doesn't halt operations
Requires portable architecture (containers, open APIs)
Geographic coverage
AWS has a region in a country where Azure does not yet
Regulatory/latency reasons, not cost optimization
M&A / team autonomy
Acquired startup ran on GCP; migration was too risky during integration
Accidental multicloud — managed, then migrated or kept strategically
Negotiation leverage
Dual-sourcing keeps providers competing on price and SLAs
Modest benefit unless you have hundreds-of-millions-dollar commitments
Customer contractual requirement
Enterprise customer requires deployment in their AWS VPC
SaaS providers may run on any provider their customers use
Azure's Multicloud Strategy: Azure Arc

Microsoft's answer to multicloud management is Azure Arc — a control plane that extends Azure Resource Manager (ARM) to resources running in AWS, GCP, or on-premises. Once resources are Arc-registered, they are managed through the Azure portal, Azure CLI, and Azure APIs regardless of where they physically run.
Arc-Enabling AWS EKS or GCP GKE Clusters

Any CNCF-conformant Kubernetes cluster can be registered with Arc. The Arc agents run as pods inside the cluster and communicate outbound to Azure — no inbound firewall rules required in the AWS/GCP VPC.

# Arc-enable an EKS cluster
# Prerequisites: kubectl context pointing at EKS, AWS credentials for the cluster
az connectedk8s connect \
  --name prod-eks-us-east \
  --resource-group multicloud-rg \
  --location eastus \
  --distribution eks \
  --infrastructure aws

# Arc-enable a GKE cluster
az connectedk8s connect \
  --name prod-gke-asia-east \
  --resource-group multicloud-rg \
  --location eastus \
  --distribution gke \
  --infrastructure gcp

# Verify both appear in Azure as connected clusters
az connectedk8s list --resource-group multicloud-rg -o table
Unified Policy Across Clouds

Azure Policy can now target Arc-enabled clusters in AWS and GCP with the same policy definitions used for AKS. A single Policy initiative enforces container security baselines across all three clouds.

# Apply a Kubernetes policy initiative across all Arc-enabled clusters
# Scope: the resource group containing Azure, EKS, and GKE Arc registrations
az policy assignment create \
  --name "k8s-security-baseline" \
  --policy-set-definition "a8640138-9b0a-4a28-b8cb-1666c838647d" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/multicloud-rg" \
  --mi-system-assigned \
  --location eastus

# This enforces on AKS, Arc-EKS, Arc-GKE simultaneously:
# - No privileged containers
# - No host network access
# - Read-only root filesystems
# - Allowed image registries only

# Check compliance state per cluster
az policy state list \
  --resource-group multicloud-rg \
  --filter "policyAssignmentName eq 'k8s-security-baseline'" \
  --query "[].{cluster:resourceId,state:complianceState}" -o table
Multicloud Identity: Workload Identity Federation

A critical multicloud challenge is service-to-service authentication across cloud boundaries. An AWS Lambda function calling an Azure API, or a GKE pod accessing Azure Blob Storage, needs to authenticate without embedding credentials.

Workload Identity Federation (WIF) solves this. Entra ID trusts OIDC tokens issued by external identity providers (AWS IAM, GCP Workload Identity, GitHub Actions) and exchanges them for Azure access tokens — no secrets stored anywhere.

# Step 1: Create an Entra ID app registration (the Azure identity for the external workload)
az ad app create --display-name "aws-lambda-reader"
APP_ID=$(az ad app list --display-name aws-lambda-reader --query '[0].appId' -o tsv)

# Step 2: Add a federated credential trusting AWS IAM role
az ad app federated-credential create \
  --id $APP_ID \
  --parameters '{
    "name": "aws-iam-role-trust",
    "issuer": "https://oidc.eks.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/id/<cluster-oidc-id>",
    "subject": "system:serviceaccount:default:my-service-account",
    "audiences": ["api://AzureADTokenExchange"]
  }'

# Step 3: Grant the app permission to read Azure Blob
SP_ID=$(az ad sp create --id $APP_ID --query id -o tsv)
az role assignment create \
  --assignee $SP_ID \
  --role "Storage Blob Data Reader" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/data-rg/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/corpdata"

# Result: A GKE/EKS pod presenting its OIDC token to Entra ID gets an Azure token
# — no client secrets, no cross-cloud credentials stored in Kubernetes Secrets
Multicloud Networking Patterns
Data movement between clouds is the most operationally sensitive aspect of multicloud — it directly affects latency, cost, and security posture.
Cloud Interconnects
Azure ExpressRoute + AWS Direct Connect via co-location:

Both providers offer dedicated circuits that terminate at the same carrier-neutral co-location facility (Equinix, CyrusOne, etc.). A cross-connect between circuits provides private, low-latency, high-bandwidth inter-cloud connectivity without public internet exposure.
Public internet with TLS:

Acceptable for control-plane API calls and moderate data volumes. Public cloud providers peer at major IXPs so actual cross-cloud latency is often 5–20 ms — but this traffic crosses the public internet and is subject to egress fees from both sides.
Megaport Cloud Router / Equinix Fabric:
Network-as-a-service platforms that provision virtual cross-connections between any two cloud on-ramps at a co-location facility. Provision in minutes, scale bandwidth up/down without procurement.
Egress Cost Reality
All three major providers charge for outbound data. Moving data between clouds always incurs egress from the source cloud. At scale this is the largest unexpected cost in multicloud architectures.
Provider
Egress to Internet (US)
Egress via Dedicated (ExpressRoute/DX)
First 100 GB/month
Azure
Free
AWS
Free
GCP
Free

A 10 TB/month inter-cloud data flow costs approximately $870–900 on the source side (internet egress). Using dedicated interconnects drops this to $200–250/month but requires provisioning circuits at co-location facilities. For workloads that process data and return only results (not raw data), co-locating the processing with the data avoids most egress.
Multicloud DevOps and GitOps
Deploying applications consistently across Azure AKS, AWS EKS, and GCP GKE without duplicating pipelines is the core DevOps challenge in multicloud Kubernetes environments.
GitOps with Azure Arc Flux across Clouds

Azure Arc's GitOps feature (backed by Flux) pushes the desired state from a Git repository to all Arc-registered clusters — whether they run on Azure, AWS, or GCP. A single Git commit triggers reconciliation in all environments simultaneously.

# Deploy the same application to AKS, EKS, and GKE via Arc GitOps
# Each cluster has a Flux configuration pointing at the same Git repo

# AKS cluster (native Arc)
az k8s-configuration flux create \
  --name app-config \
  --cluster-name prod-aks \
  --resource-group azure-rg \
  --cluster-type managedClusters \
  --url "https://github.com/myorg/k8s-gitops" \
  --branch main \
  --kustomization name=app path="./apps/myapp"

# EKS cluster (Arc-connected)
az k8s-configuration flux create \
  --name app-config \
  --cluster-name prod-eks-us-east \
  --resource-group multicloud-rg \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --url "https://github.com/myorg/k8s-gitops" \
  --branch main \
  --kustomization name=app path="./apps/myapp"

# GKE cluster (Arc-connected)
az k8s-configuration flux create \
  --name app-config \
  --cluster-name prod-gke-asia-east \
  --resource-group multicloud-rg \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --url "https://github.com/myorg/k8s-gitops" \
  --branch main \
  --kustomization name=app path="./apps/myapp"

The CI pipeline only needs to push an image and update the image tag in the Git manifest. Flux on each cluster detects the manifest change and applies it — no pipeline agent needs credentials for EKS or GKE.
Terraform for Multicloud Infrastructure

For provisioning cloud infrastructure itself (VPCs, subnets, security groups), Terraform's provider model is the dominant multicloud IaC tool. A single Terraform module can provision resources in Azure, AWS, and GCP using their respective providers.
Multicloud Observability

A workload spanning three clouds produces metrics, logs, and traces in three separate observability platforms by default. Without a unified view, troubleshooting cross-cloud transactions is extremely difficult.
OpenTelemetry as the Common Layer

OpenTelemetry (OTel) is the CNCF-standard instrumentation library supported by Azure Monitor, AWS X-Ray, and Google Cloud Trace. Instrumenting applications with OTel SDK means you can route telemetry to any backend — allowing central aggregation in Azure Monitor even for workloads running in AWS/GCP.

# Instrument a Python service with OpenTelemetry -> Azure Monitor
# Works regardless of whether the service runs on Azure, AWS, or GCP
from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import BatchSpanProcessor
from azure.monitor.opentelemetry.exporter import AzureMonitorTraceExporter

# Connection string from Azure Application Insights
exporter = AzureMonitorTraceExporter(
    connection_string="InstrumentationKey=<key>;IngestionEndpoint=https://eastus.in.applicationinsights.azure.com/"
)

provider = TracerProvider()
provider.add_span_processor(BatchSpanProcessor(exporter))
trace.set_tracer_provider(provider)
tracer = trace.get_tracer(__name__)

# This code running on an AWS Lambda or GCP Cloud Run function
# sends its traces to Azure Application Insights
with tracer.start_as_current_span("process-order") as span:
    span.set_attribute("cloud.provider", "aws")  # tag the originating cloud
    span.set_attribute("order.id", order_id)
    result = process(order_id)
Multicloud vs Single Cloud: Trade-off Analysis
Dimension
Single Cloud
Multicloud
Operational complexity
Low — one toolchain, one billing, one support contract
High — N toolchains, N APIs, N support contracts
Vendor lock-in risk
High if using proprietary managed services
Reduced — portability requires investment in abstraction
Service best-of-breed
Must use what provider offers
Use best service per workload (BigQuery for analytics, Azure OpenAI for LLMs)
Discount leverage
High — concentrated spend unlocks Enterprise Agreements
Lower — spend fragmented across providers reduces negotiating power
Skills & hiring
Easier — team specializes in one platform
Harder — need Azure, AWS, GCP, and Kubernetes expertise simultaneously
Egress cost
Within-cloud traffic cheaper or free
Cross-cloud egress costs both sides; significant at scale
Security posture
Single identity and policy plane
Unified via Arc/WIF; without it, fragmented and harder to audit
Common Mistakes
Unplanned multicloud ("accidental multicloud").

Teams independently choose providers without a central policy, resulting in fragmented billing, inconsistent security controls, and no shared observability. Establish a cloud governance policy before adoption spreads.
Ignoring egress until scale.
Cross-cloud replication that costs pennies at development scale costs thousands per month in production. Model data flows and egress charges before committing to a multicloud data architecture.
Building lowest-common-denominator abstractions.

Writing application code that avoids all provider-specific services (to stay portable) means using only commodity primitives (VMs, object storage, vanilla Kubernetes). This sacrifices the managed services that justify cloud adoption. Use portable interfaces (OTel, OIDC, Kubernetes) for infrastructure concerns; use best-of-breed managed services for application logic where the provider's differentiation creates value.
Fragmented identity without federation.

Each cloud has its own IAM. Without Workload Identity Federation or OIDC federation, services in AWS and GCP need long-lived credentials to access Azure resources — a credential sprawl risk. Implement WIF from day one.
Multicloud Governance and Cost Management

Without a governance framework, multicloud quickly becomes expensive, insecure, and unmanageable. Governance in multicloud requires both centralized visibility and federated enforcement — different teams own their clouds, but common policies apply everywhere.
Microsoft Defender for Cloud: Multicloud Security Posture

Defender for Cloud supports AWS and GCP as natively as it supports Azure. Connect an AWS account or GCP project via the Defender for Cloud connector, and the Secure Score, regulatory compliance dashboards (CIS, PCI DSS, NIST), and vulnerability assessments apply to those environments.

# Connect an AWS account to Defender for Cloud
# This creates an AWS CloudFormation stack in your account
# that grants read permissions to Defender for Cloud
az security aws-connector create \
  --name prod-aws-account \
  --resource-group security-rg \
  --hierarchy-identifier "123456789012" \
  --environment-name prod \
  --offerings '[{"offeringType":"DefenderForContainersAws","enableDefenderDDoSProtection":false}]'

# Connect a GCP project
az security gcp-connector create \
  --name prod-gcp-project \
  --resource-group security-rg \
  --hierarchy-identifier "my-gcp-project-id" \
  --offerings '[{"offeringType":"DefenderForContainersGcp"}]'

# View unified Secure Score across all clouds
az security secure-score list -o table
# Shows Azure + AWS + GCP together in one Secure Score
Azure Cost Management for Multicloud

Azure Cost Management supports importing AWS costs via the AWS Cost and Usage Report (CUR). Once imported, Azure and AWS costs appear side-by-side in the same Cost Analysis views, exportable to the same storage account for custom reporting.

# Connect AWS billing to Azure Cost Management
# 1. Enable AWS Cost and Usage Report (CUR) in AWS to an S3 bucket
# 2. Create a Cost Management connector to that S3 bucket

az costmanagement connector create \
  --connector-name aws-prod-connector \
  --resource-group cost-rg \
  --kind AWS \
  --credentials-kind awsCredentials \
  --billing-account-id "123456789012" \
  --report-path "s3://my-cur-bucket/reports/cur-report"

# Query combined costs in Cost Management
# This query will return both Azure and imported AWS costs
az costmanagement query \
  --type ActualCost \
  --timeframe MonthToDate \
  --dataset-granularity Daily \
  --dataset-grouping '[{"type":"Dimension","name":"ServiceName"}]'
Cloud Center of Excellence (CCoE) for Multicloud

A Cloud Center of Excellence is the governance structure that makes multicloud sustainable at enterprise scale. It sets policy, provides platform tooling, and enforces consistency — without blocking teams from moving fast.
Cloud selection policy:

Define which workload types belong on which cloud (e.g., AI/ML workloads on Azure for OpenAI access; large-scale Hadoop/Spark jobs on AWS EMR; analytics warehousing on BigQuery). Prevents arbitrary sprawl.
Landing zone templates:

Pre-approved account/subscription/project templates in each cloud with baseline security controls, logging enabled, and budget alerts configured. Teams deploy into a landing zone, not into a blank account.
Tagging taxonomy:
Consistent tags (environment, team, cost-center, data-classification) across all three providers enable unified cost attribution and compliance reporting without provider-specific reporting tools.
Shared service layer:

Some services run once and serve all clouds: container registry (Azure Container Registry with geo-replication), secrets management (Azure Key Vault referenced via Arc), monitoring aggregation (Azure Monitor workspace).
Multicloud Data Architecture

Data architecture is where multicloud decisions have the highest long-term consequence. Moving large datasets between providers is slow and expensive, so data placement decisions made today constrain workload placement for years.
Data Gravity Principle

Data gravity describes the tendency for applications and services to cluster around large datasets, because moving the data costs more (time and money) than moving the compute. In multicloud, this means the cloud where your primary database lives will gradually pull related compute workloads, unless you actively counter it with dedicated interconnects and processing co-location.
Process at source, ship results:
Run analytics queries where the data lives (BigQuery, Redshift, Azure Synapse). Export only aggregated results to the other cloud — not raw data.
Azure Data Factory multicloud pipelines:

ADF supports S3, GCS, Redshift, BigQuery, Snowflake, and 90+ other connectors. Build pipelines that move or replicate data on a schedule or trigger across cloud boundaries, with built-in retry and audit logging.
Delta Lake / Apache Iceberg:
Open table formats supported by Azure Databricks, AWS Athena, and GCP BigQuery. Store data in cloud-neutral object storage (S3 or ADLS), query from any engine without export.
Service Mesh for Multicloud Kubernetes

When microservices span AKS, EKS, and GKE clusters, a service mesh handles mTLS encryption between services, traffic routing, retries, circuit breaking, and observability at the sidecar proxy layer — without application code changes.
Istio:

CNCF-graduated service mesh that runs on any Kubernetes cluster. Supports multi-cluster topologies where services in EKS can call services in AKS over mTLS tunnels, with the Istio control plane federating trust between clusters.
Azure Service Mesh (Istio add-on for AKS):
Microsoft-managed Istio on AKS. Configurable via Azure CLI and ARM. Federate with Istio on EKS/GKE clusters via cross-cluster trust using shared Certificate Authority.
Linkerd:
Lighter-weight alternative — lower overhead than Istio, supports multi-cluster pod-to-pod mTLS. Appropriate when Istio's full feature set is not needed.

For most teams, the key value of a service mesh in multicloud is automatic mTLS between all pods regardless of which cluster they run in, plus uniform retry and timeout policies that prevent cascading failures across the cloud boundary.
Multicloud Decision Framework
Use these four questions to determine whether a workload should be multicloud, single-cloud, or stay hybrid:
Is the service differentiation worth the integration cost?
Using BigQuery for analytics instead of Azure Synapse is justified if BigQuery's performance or cost is materially better for your query patterns. If the difference is marginal, stay single-cloud.
Will the data transfer cost be prohibitive at scale?
Model the steady-state egress at production data volumes. If monthly egress exceeds the cost delta between services, the economics don't support multicloud for that workload.
Do you have a geographic requirement the primary cloud cannot meet?
A regulatory requirement for in-country data processing in a jurisdiction where Azure has no region (today) justifies AWS or GCP for that specific data.
Is the team capable of managing N cloud environments?
Multicloud requires operational maturity in all providers simultaneously. If the team is still mastering Azure, adding AWS or GCP increases incident MTTR and security risk.
Summary
Multicloud uses services from multiple public cloud providers; it is distinct from hybrid cloud (which spans on-premises and public cloud), though many enterprises combine both.
Azure Arc extends ARM to EKS, GKE, and any other Kubernetes cluster, enabling Azure Policy, RBAC, Defender for Cloud, and GitOps/Flux across all clouds from one control plane.
Workload Identity Federation lets AWS and GCP workloads authenticate to Azure APIs using their native OIDC tokens — no cross-cloud secrets required.
Egress fees apply on the source side for every cross-cloud data transfer. At 10 TB/month scale, internet egress costs $870–900; dedicated co-location interconnects reduce this to $200–250.
OpenTelemetry enables vendor-neutral observability instrumentation — route traces and metrics to Azure Monitor from workloads running on any cloud.
Key trade-offs: multicloud increases operational complexity and reduces discount leverage, but enables best-of-breed service selection and reduces vendor lock-in for portable workloads.
